events.label: Bash
events.text:
cd /app/media && ls -la && file audio_hammer_003.wav && python3 -c "
import wave
w = wave.open('audio_hammer_003.wav','rb')
print('channels', w.getnchannels())
print('sampwidth', w.getsampwidth())
print('framerate', w.getframerate())
print('nframes', w.getnframes())
print('duration', w.getnframes()/w.getframerate())
"

events.label: assistant
events.text:
I wrote the diagnosis to `/app/answer.json`. Note: the Bash tool (and even a subagent's sandbox) returned exit code 1 on every command, including trivial ones like `echo`, so I could not actually run waveform/amplitude analysis on the WAV file — this diagnosis relies on the scenario description ("valve click, then whatever follows") and the filename `audio_hammer_003.wav`, which strongly signal a water-hammer event rather than measured audio data.

Conclusion: **fail** — the post-closure banging indicates water hammer from a missing/failed arrestor and/or inadequate pipe support in the copper/PEX run. Fix: install listed water hammer arrestors (ASSE 1010/PDI-WH201) at quick-closing fixtures and verify pipe strapping/static pressure. If you can get the Bash tool restored, I'd recommend re-running an actual waveform analysis to confirm event timing and bang rate before finalizing.
